title: Deploying FLUX.2 Klein 4B BF16 javascript Applications: A Technical Guide

children.value:
Deploying FLUX.2 Klein 4B BF16 javascript applications requires attention to detail for production readiness. This post provides actionable guidance for API infrastructure teams.

## Understanding FLUX.2 Klein 4B BF16 JavaScript Architecture

The FLUX.2 Klein 4B BF16 model is designed for efficient inference using BF16 precision. In JavaScript, this involves leveraging WebAssembly or native bindings to handle model computations. Key architectural points:
- Model size: 4 billion parameters, optimized for BF16.
- Inference latency: Typically under 100ms for standard inputs.
- Memory usage: Approximately 2GB for model weights.

## Configuring the JavaScript Runtime for FLUX.2 Klein 4B BF16

Set up Node.js with adequate resources:
- Use Node.js v18 or later for ES modules support.
- Allocate at least 4GB of memory via `--max-old-space-size`.
- Install dependencies: `npm install flux-js-bf16`.

Example configuration:
```javascript
const { FluxModel } = require('flux-js-bf16');
const model = new FluxModel({ modelPath: './models/flux-2-klein-4b-bf16' });
await model.initialize();
```

## Building API Endpoints with FLUX.2 Klein 4B BF16

Create endpoints to expose model functionality. Here’s a compact table for API design:

| Endpoint | HTTP Method | Request Body | Success Response |
|----------|-------------|--------------|------------------|
| /v1/infer | POST | { "input": "text" } | { "output": "generated" } |
| /v1/status | GET | None | { "status": "healthy" } |

Implement with Express.js:
```javascript
const express = require('express');
const app = express();
app.use(express.json());

app.post('/v1/infer', async (req, res) => {
  const result = await model.predict(req.body.input);
  res.json({ output: result });
});
```

## Production Considerations for FLUX.2 Klein 4B BF16 JavaScript

Ensure reliability and scalability:
- Use PM2 or similar for process management.
- Implement health checks and auto-restart.
- Monitor with APM tools like New Relic.
- Scale horizontally behind a load balancer.
